active_question: What is the Japanese term and English translation for the 'greeting ties' featured in Part II?
edited_answer: the overall readiness of your rope partner for an emotional exchange
edited_question: According to the tutorial announcement, what is Aisatsu shibari designed to gauge?
reason: The edit replaces a bilingual term lookup with the source's stated relational purpose while keeping the claim explicitly attributed.
support_evidence: Aisatsu shibari (greeting shibari) is designed to gauge the overall readiness of your rope partner for an emotional exchange and, therefore, for a deeply satisfying session. As with other Yukimura-based techniques, these ties are designed to gently open a dialogue with your partner to establish the most appropriate approach and to align the session with their desires to maximise the emotional exchange.

active_question: What starting position is recommended to give the maximum range of motion for twisting the body?
reason: A prescriptive spinal-torsion starting position lacks medical qualification, screening, range-of-motion limits, monitoring, and stop criteria.
support_evidence: Twisting the body is a fun way to create restriction, get the shoulder in one direction, get the hips in the opposite direction. This is more difficult than it sounds. An important part is to get the spine straight as a starting position. This will give the maximum range of motion to twist the body into position. We’ll dive into more torsion positions on Day 166.

So he told us that it is always the rope bottom that will lose the feeling of connection first. Such a digression might be special to a private lesson, and it was fun.

active_question: What device can be used in case of emergency if you are having difficulty untying the knot?
edited_answer: with a lot of pressure, the knot can compact and become difficult to untie, especially when the tail is under tension
edited_question: What emergency-relevant limitation does Rope365 give for the Somerville bowline?
reason: The edit replaces a generic cutting-device answer with the knot condition that can make emergency release difficult.
support_evidence: The biggest con of this knot is that it is a bit more difficult to learn, take your time to visualize the different steps and you will master it in no time. If you are struggling, don’t hesitate to explore other topics and come back to it when you feel ready. Another downside is that with a lot of pressure, the knot can compact and become difficult to untie, especially when the tail is under tension. In case of emergency, you can always use your safety cutting device if you are having difficulty untying the knot.

reason: A joining-knot recommendation from a source framed as one contributor's adaptations lacks validated load, tail, and failure limits.
support_evidence: The benefits of the last bend; it has the joining ability of a triple-fisherman while being easy to untie, it is also an easy pattern to evolve from the double-coin knot, which is a common decorative knot we use in rope bondage.

active_question: What is the name of the classic tie illustrated by Nureki in full kimono in the video?
reason: A tie-identification question from a commercial streaming-video announcement is promotional and lacks usable safety context.
support_evidence: Nureki’s videos often focus on traditional Japanese clothing or costumes, creating a very traditional feel to his rope. In this tutorial, he illustrates a classic Ebi (shrimp) tie, done in full kimono, first tied seated and then as a full suspension.

active_question: Which specific nerve located in the elbow is identified as a risk when using the second rope?
reason: The immediately preceding audited tranche already retains the same source's ulnar-nerve-at-the-elbow fact with fuller context.
support_evidence: Continuous monitoring of hand sensation and movement is essential to mitigate nerve issues. The risks at the wrists and at the base of the deltoid from the first rope are still present. With the second rope, we add risk of injuring the brachial plexus in the armpit and the ulnar nerve in the elbow. By tying loosely and avoiding bulk between the arm and the chest, we reduce a lot of those risks. With experience, you’ll also find the best placement for the person in rope, each person is unique. Fell free to skip ahead and read more about nerves in Day 50.

active_question: Which types of ropes are described as easy to wash and can be washed after each use?
reason: The source directly distinguishes easy-to-wash synthetic and cotton ropes from natural fibers weakened by wet cleaning.
support_evidence: We generally want to wash our rope only if needed. Synthetic and cotton ropes are easy to wash, so they can be washed after each use. Hemp and jute gets weaker when wet, and will take time drying under tension. If you decide to wash these rope more often, you might have to replace your kit sooner.

active_question: What is the recommended diameter for cotton or nylon rope when starting out at the hardware store?
reason: A fixed hardware-store buying prescription omits construction, chemical treatment, load, use case, inspection, and supplier-quality considerations.
support_evidence: - Answer 4: Go to the hardware store, take cotton or nylon rope 6mm (1/4 inch), then cut into several pieces around 8m (between 7-10m). The reality is that even if it is not “your perfect rope”, nylon and cotton ropes are really cheap, easy to wash and good enough to have a fun time.

active_question: What specific friction modification is suggested for the unlocked top wrap of an X-friction when using low-tooth rope?
reason: An isolated modification to a potentially loaded harness lacks placement, loading, monitoring, and release context.
support_evidence: The x-friction is used to lock wraps vertically on the stem, but with low-tooth rope, the top wrap is generally not locked and the friction can shift significantly along the stem. I modify the X-friction by adding a L-friction to the unlocked top wrap.

active_question: What specific knot is mentioned as a good way to mark the middle of the rope for later reuse?
reason: The answer concerns a non-body rope-handling use and the source explicitly notes that slip knots themselves are unstable.
support_evidence: | Day 218: Slip Knots – Slip knots are fast, they tighten and they have multiple fun uses if we take into the fact that they are not stable. The basic slip knot can be used for quick capture, and a noose can create an adjustable loop. Because it’s quite fast to tie, a quick slip knot is a good way to mark the middle of the rope in case you want to use your rope again later. |

active_question: What is the term for how far apart two points of touching must be to be perceived apart from one unit?
reason: The source directly defines a standard tactile-acuity concept and situates it as an observation about differing touch sensitivity.
support_evidence: The density of the mechanoreceptors in the skin changes across the body. The hands and mouths have much more than the rest of the body. We can measure this density by measuring the two-point threshold, which is how far apart two points of touching must be to be perceived apart from one unit. On the fingers, two ropes next to each other will feel like two distinct ropes while wraps of rope on the chest will feel like one large band… unless there is a big gap between them. We’ll also be able to feel rope tension more accurately anywhere we have higher mechanoreceptor density.

active_question: What specific anatomical structure is identified as running in the location of the forearm pressure points where heavy pressure may create damage?
reason: The claim appears inside prescriptive pain-compliance instructions and does not adequately establish anatomical location or safe application.
support_evidence: A bit more difficult to execute, but quite elegant, is to use the pressure points on the forearm. The idea is to create a lever effect where the body reflex will be to get the wrist up and the elbow down, and from there the whole body will have to get down on the floor. Grab the arm, place your fingers underneath to create a stable pivot point, then extend the thumb and reach out to one of the pressure points on the top of the forearm. Steady light pressure will be sufficient to activate the reflex of fear of pain and the body will naturally react. If we remember the pathways from Day 50, the ulnar nerve is running in this location, heavy pressure or impact may create damage as we are interfering with nerves.

active_question: Which hitch is described as a very popular hitch to use in situations requiring a bit of friction to help maintain control?
edited_answer: The Munter hitch was originally designed to be used in belay systems to add friction on moving rope, while the crossing hitch is designed to hold things in place and prevent movement
edited_question: How does Rope365 distinguish the purpose of a Munter hitch from a crossing hitch?
reason: The edit replaces a name lookup with the source's functional distinction between two often-confused hitches.
support_evidence: | Day 213: Friction Hitches – Sometimes we want to have a pulley system with just a bit of friction to help to maintain control and prevent the rope from sliding too fast without locking completely. The Munter hitch is a very popular hitch to use in those situations. Because of the similar topology between the Munter and the crossing hitch from Day 10, many people see them as the same hitch. The Munter hitch was originally designed to be used in belay systems to add friction on moving rope, while the crossing hitch is designed to hold things in place and prevent movement. |

active_question: What is the traditional Japanese name for the Open Knees tie, which is described as a traditional Japanese gate at a Shinto temple?
reason: The source directly pairs the Japanese term and characters with the named open-knees pose and torii-gate gloss.
support_evidence: | Day 156: Open Knees – Alt M-shape, torii shibari 鳥居縛り (traditional Japanese gate at Shinto temple). Create a tie that will keep the knees open. A great way to do that is by tying knees together with the rope going in the back in between. You can also use a chest harness and attach it to each side. The variation with a the knee connected via the back in a straight line is often refered to as moon tie. Inspirations and Resources |

active_question: Which nerve runs very superficially over the boney lump on the outside of the leg and supplies sensation and motor functions down to the foot?
edited_answer: keep the ropes on your muscles, away from the boney structures around your knee caps
edited_question: What placement precaution does the source give after locating the common peroneal nerve near the knee?
reason: The edit makes the source's practical nerve-avoidance precaution retrievable instead of only asking for the nerve's name.
support_evidence: There is one particular nerve to be aware of when it comes to futomomos. Approximately four fingers diagonally down on the outside of the leg from the edge of your kneecap, there is a little boney lump. Take a moment to palpate it together with the one tying you, and you will probably discover that pressing on it can create some unpleasant sensations running down your leg. What you feel is the common peroneal nerve, and it supplies both sensation and motor functions all the way down to your foot. It runs very superficially over this boney lump, and even though you are unlikely to hurt it while working on the floor, it is better to take the habit to avoid putting rope there. Help the one tying you to remember to keep the ropes on your muscles, away from the boney structures around your knee caps.
